topic: Someone's childhood imaginary friend appears in their niece's drawings
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the stack of watercolor sketches spread across her desk, the afternoon light catching the edges of the paper in a way that made the shadows seem to shift. Her niece, Lily, had been drawing fantastical creatures lately—winged things with too many eyes and creatures with impossible limbs. But tucked into the corner of one drawing, almost obscured by a splash of bright blue, was a figure. It was tall, slender, with hair the exact shade of faded copper she remembered from her own childhood. A cold knot tightened in Elara’s stomach. She picked up a pencil, her hand trembling slightly before she dared to trace the outline of the figure. It looked familiar, deeply, unsettlingly so. Every time she looked away, she felt a prickling sensation on the back of her neck, as if something unseen was watching her movements behind her chair. She quickly flipped the page, but the image seemed to linger in her vision, mocking her attempts to dismiss it as a mere childhood echo.

[story 2]

"Are you sure you locked the back door, Aunt Clara?" Lily asked, her voice bright and oblivious as she handed over a sketchbook. Clara watched the exchange from the doorway, her gaze snagging on the page Lily presented. There, sketched in charcoal, was a boy with wide, luminous eyes and a perpetually mischievous grin—the exact depiction of Barnaby, the imaginary companion she’d conjured up when she was seven. Clara felt a sudden, sharp need to check the deadbolt, her fingers fumbling with the brass knob. The air in the room suddenly felt thick, heavy, pressing down on her chest. She moved to the window, peering out at the quiet suburban street, half-expecting to see a shadow detach itself from the glass. When she turned back, the drawing remained, vibrant and undeniably present, and Clara felt a profound sense of being observed, scrutinized by something that existed only on paper.

[story 3]

I kept glancing at the collection of Lily’s artwork, my jaw tight. It wasn't just the whimsical nature of the drawings that bothered me; it was the specificity. In the corner of the latest piece, a small, vaguely defined shape appeared—a silhouette that mirrored the one from my own forgotten years. It was the shape of Finn, the silent, shadow-walking companion I’d invented as a child. My breath hitched. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the living room, my footsteps loud on the hardwood floor. Each time I stopped, I scanned the room, convinced that the air itself was vibrating with an unseen presence. I forced myself to look back at the paper, trying to rationalize it as coincidence, a trick of the light, but the way that shape seemed to lean toward the edge of the page, almost looking *out*, sent a shiver of profound unease down my spine.
